decomposition.title: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise solutions, kitchen and bath products, wellness, hospitality, and digital innovation
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred $5M-$15M+ potential opportunity based on enterprise-scale hosting evaluation for data center exit, large VMware/physical estate, SAP ecosystem, Windows/Linux servers, databases, storage, backup/DR, network connectivity, monitoring/logging/security, and pricing requested for hosting, migration/implementation if applicable, consulting/support/managed services. [PAGE 4], [PAGE 6], [PAGE 9]
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated in the document. Inferred likely 3-5 years for any subsequent hosting/DCaaS/cloud transformation or managed-services contracting phase, while this RFP itself is a Phase 0 evaluation and does not commit to execution. requirements.text: Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing operating model and support framework.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
Evaluation should ensure application performance and availability are maintained or improved.
Evaluation should ensure integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported.
Evaluation should ensure transition risks and business disruption are minimized.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities/shared responsibility framework.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including governance, security, and compliance approach.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including operations and support model.
Proposed models should demonstrate how they can support a progressive transition away from the current Data Center footprint while maintaining business continuity and operational stability.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears inside the Scope of Work rather than in legal terms; this is a common hidden disqualifier area, but the document only requires an approach, not a specific residency jurisdiction.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: No specific required data residency location is stated in the provided document.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, Cloud, DCaaS, and/or Hybrid, where applicable and clearly state assumptions.
In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.

In-scope services include COTS and internally developed applications, Windows and Linux virtual machines, databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL and other supported platforms, middleware and integration platforms, file storage systems, backup and disaster recovery solutions, identity and access management integrations, network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments, monitoring, logging and security tooling, and selected OT-adjacent workloads limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory certification evidence requirement is in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements subsection and references unspecified applicable industry/regional standards, creating a risk of missing required evidence in the response.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required, specific certifications not named.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory requirement applies specifically to DCaaS providers and points to more information in the Vendor Response template, which is not in the main RFP text.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to review, validate, and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory dataset is not in the provided document, but it is mandatory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data gaps must be called out; failure to do so could undermine pricing/sizing acceptance because the inventory is the stated baseline.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The provided inventory must be used as baseline; alternative sizing without alignment may make the response non-comparable.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; RFP responses/proposals are due to Kohler on 26 June.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission timing/channel is procedural and can eliminate a proposal before content evaluation.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The named recipients are the designated communication channel; missed copy recipient could create submission compliance risk.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory response template is a common administrative disqualifier and references an attachment not included in this document.

requirements.text: Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A superficially complete proposal that is not aligned to each requirement may be treated as non-comparable/non-responsive.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP explicitly says simple Yes/No answers will not be sufficient; this can invalidate otherwise compliant template responses.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Separate SAP/non-SAP cost views are easy to miss but important for commercial comparability and business case evaluation.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency requirement is a mandatory pricing format requirement; non-USD commercial submissions may be non-compliant.

requirements.text: Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 is not stated as automatic rejection, but less than net 90 will not be considered competitive and could materially weaken or effectively disqualify the proposal commercially.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.

Governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework for issue resolution, and key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality terms are in the appendix and can create pre-award legal/compliance exposure if ignored.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Third-party/subcontractor sharing restriction is buried in appendix legal terms and requires prior written consent.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Broad legal/compliance capability expectation is located in the appendix, with detailed terms deferred to later phases.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments; no specific jurisdiction stated.

similar_deals.title: Pharma IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Comparable enterprise IT transformation caution. Directly relevant because Kohler requires detailed commercial and pricing structure, and the opportunity may be price-sensitive despite technical complexity.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 42%. Evidence supporting a credible bid: past proposal intelligence contains multiple exact or near-exact Project Polaris patterns, especially RFP-81E4E8, RFP-3E0EFE, RFP-9F4932, RFP-8D620E, RFP-1A1C8C, and RFP-FB30BB, showing strong reusable solution fit around a 12-week vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, workload-by-workload placement, SAP/VMware dependency mapping, open-book USD TCO, landing-zone architecture, migration waves, rollback gates, and operating model. Deal corpus also shows wins in adjacent manufacturing/SAP/enterprise transformation contexts, including DEAL-003, DEAL-038, and DEAL-094, where SAP credibility, manufacturing domain proof, proprietary IP, and trust reduced perceived risk. However, the probability is held below 50% for four reasons: first, no public procurement data is available to validate actual competitor pricing or award patterns, so confidence is only medium; second, the strongest past proposal records do not provide outcomes, so they prove fit but not that we have won Kohler or this specific type of award; third, past intelligence flags Cognizant as having a renewed multi-year partnership in core digital foundation domains, creating a meaningful incumbent advantage; fourth, this is a Phase 0 evaluation with no committed downstream transformation, and Kohler’s detailed commercial/compliance/template requirements create rejection or scoring risk if execution is imperfect. If we can prove compliance attestations, submit a precise USD modular price, demonstrate manufacturing/SAP references, and neutralize incumbent risk through a coexistence-first operating model, win probability could improve into the high-40s. Without those fixes, probability would fall toward the low-30s.
capability_gaps: Requirement gap: Kohler requires evidence of certifications and compliance capability for hosting workloads; before submission, the team must attach current ISO 27001, SOC 2, regional compliance, cloud partner, and any DCaaS compliance attestations rather than making unsupported claims.

Requirement gap: Kohler requires target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing-zone principles; proposal must include concrete Azure/AWS/GCP landing-zone reference architecture, environment segregation for Prod/Test/Dev, IAM, network segmentation, logging, backup/DR, and security control mappings specific to Kohler’s SAP, VMware, Windows/Linux, database, and manufacturing estate.

Requirement gap: Kohler requires support for enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem integration; proposal must show how plant/edge connectivity, SAP integrations, OT-adjacent dependencies, latency-sensitive workloads, and global manufacturing/business continuity will be assessed and protected.

Requirement gap: Kohler requires operating model, shared responsibility framework, governance, security, compliance, and support model; proposal must include a detailed RACI including Kohler, incumbent providers, cloud/DCaaS providers, security operations, network teams, application teams, and managed services teams.

Requirement gap: Kohler requires commercial and pricing structure in USD; proposal must provide modular, itemized pricing for Phase 0 advisory, optional migration/implementation, hosting/DCaaS/cloud run costs, backup/DR, network connectivity, security monitoring, support, and managed services. Prior loss evidence shows pricing 20% above competitors can lose even with a stronger solution.

Requirement gap: Kohler requires transition risks and business disruption to be minimized; proposal must include migration wave logic, dependency mapping, rollback gates, RTO/RPO tiering, hypercare, and performance/availability validation criteria, not only a high-level migration narrative.
recommended_win_themes: Evidence-led, vendor-neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab: Position Phase 0 as a workload-by-workload decision factory comparing public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration options without forcing a predetermined migration outcome.

Manufacturing-safe SAP and enterprise continuity: Lead with SAP, VMware/VxRail, Windows/Linux, database, network, backup/DR, and plant/edge dependency expertise, emphasizing performance, availability, rollback gates, RTO/RPO tiering, and zero/low-disruption transition methods.

Kohler-owned decision evidence and no lock-in: Differentiate with open-book USD TCO scenarios, target-state architecture, dependency maps, migration waves, compliance evidence matrix, RACI, and downstream implementation package that Kohler can use with any provider.

Transparent modular commercial model: Offer clearly separated pricing for advisory assessment, migration, hosting/DCaaS/cloud run, managed services, security/monitoring, backup/DR, and optional transformation waves. This directly addresses Kohler’s commercial-transparency scoring and avoids the historical loss pattern where a superior solution lost due to being 20% overpriced.

Coexistence-first operating model: Recognize the likely incumbent/provider ecosystem and propose a practical shared-responsibility framework, service integration model, tooling integration, escalation paths, AIOps/observability layer, and governance cadence rather than implying disruptive takeover.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so pricing and competitor assumptions cannot be validated against government contract ground truth. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.

The strongest past proposal intelligence is highly relevant to this exact Kohler/Project Polaris opportunity, but no win/loss outcome is provided for those prior records; it proves reusable positioning and pricing patterns, not proven success with Kohler.

Incumbent risk is material. Past proposal intelligence notes Cognizant has a renewed multi-year partnership in core digital foundation domains, which could advantage an incumbent or existing ecosystem provider unless our response is explicitly coexistence-oriented and commercially compelling.

This RFP appears to be Phase 0 evaluation only and does not guarantee downstream hosting, migration, or managed-services award. Pursuit economics should treat the initial award as strategic entry, not a committed $15M-$50M transformation.

Commercial risk is high because Kohler asks for detailed pricing and USD transparency. Deal corpus includes a comparable enterprise IT transformation loss where a technically better proposal lost because pricing was 20% above the winning bid.
Compliance and attestation risk is explicit. Missing certification evidence or DCaaS compliance attestations could materially reduce scoring or create rejection risk.

Operational-model risk is high because Kohler requires target operating model, governance, security, compliance, support framework, shared responsibility, and business-continuity protection. A generic cloud-migration response would likely score poorly.

Manufacturing disruption risk is central. Any weakness in SAP, plant/edge integration, latency, network connectivity, DR, or rollback planning will be heavily penalized because Kohler requires performance and availability to be maintained or improved.
must_fix_before_bidding: Use the Kohler Vendor Response template exactly, ensure every requirement has a detailed response, and avoid simple Yes/No answers because the RFP explicitly states these will not be sufficient.
Submit by the 26 June deadline through the required channel: Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
Attach certification evidence and DCaaS compliance attestations where applicable; unsupported compliance claims create a material rejection/scoring risk.

Finalize an open-book USD commercial model with clear assumptions, dependencies, exclusions, unit rates, and optional pricing tiers. Include a Phase 0 advisory price anchor around the prior $279,533.42 pattern if bidding only the evaluation, and separate downstream hosting/transformation options to avoid overpricing the initial decision phase.

Address incumbent coexistence explicitly. Past proposal intelligence notes Cognizant has a renewed multi-year partnership covering core digital foundation domains, so the proposal must avoid assuming greenfield control and instead define transition boundaries, collaboration model, tool integration, and escalation governance.

Include manufacturing- and SAP-specific proof points, including zero/low-disruption methods, SAP dependency mapping, VMware/VxRail disposition, database/storage/backup/DR treatment, and global plant/business-continuity safeguards.

Declare all assumptions, constraints, data dependencies, and required Kohler inputs, including workload inventory, CMDB quality, application ownership, network diagrams, RTO/RPO targets, security requirements, incumbent contracts, and cost baselines.

client_intel.recommended_narrative: Position as a workload-led hybrid hosting strategy, not a generic cloud migration. Emphasize application dependency mapping, resilient operations, privacy-grade security, observability, and FinOps governance that can coexist with Kohler’s Cognizant ecosystem.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium; confidence: medium. Evidence supports strong strategic fit but not a confirmed Kohler/Wisconsin hosting pursuit. Accenture had a Milwaukee, WI Application Lead: Business Intelligence posting and multiple SAP/infrastructure/cloud roles, but no recent public Accenture contract win in Wisconsin was provided. No recent contract wins in Wisconsin in the supplied public-contract data; Source 2 states no public procurement data available.
competitors.predicted_positioning: Accenture will likely position as the lowest-risk global transformation partner: secure multi-cloud architecture, enterprise managed services, SAP/cloud integration, OT cybersecurity, manufacturing digital core, and global operating model. Evidence: July 2026 NATO protected business network multi-cloud contract; 2026 Stellantis, Microsoft/Avanade factory, Mitsubishi Chemical, Siemens Digital Industries, and OT-security announcements; Q3 FY2026 managed-services bookings of $9.06B. Confidence: medium-high on positioning, medium on account-specific intent.
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Estimated full-scope indicative hosting/migration/managed-services proposal: $6.5M-$15.0M; confidence: low-medium because Source 2 provides no public contract award values. Grounding: RFP opportunity was user-inferred at $5M-$15M+; Accenture Q3 FY2026 operating margin was 17.0%, so pricing floor = delivery cost / 0.83 = 1.205x delivery cost. Revenue/employee calculation unavailable from supplied sources because Accenture employee count was not provided. Expect upper-half pricing because Accenture's current evidence is large-enterprise/global managed-services and secure multi-cloud work, not commodity hosting.
their_strengths: Massive enterprise managed-services scale: Q3 FY2026 managed-services revenue of $9.39B and managed-services bookings of $9.06B. Source: Accenture Q3 FY2026 earnings release/SEC filing.

Recent secure multi-cloud infrastructure proof point: NATO Communications and Information Agency Protected Business Network program for roughly 29,000 users across a multi-cloud environment. Source: Accenture newsroom, July 7, 2026.

Manufacturing relevance through Stellantis AI/digital twin, Microsoft/Avanade agentic factory, Mitsubishi Chemical operational transformation, Siemens Digital Industries capability expansion, and OT cybersecurity moves. Source: web search/newsroom intelligence.
SAP and enterprise architecture capability supported by public SAP alliance messaging and SAP Architect/SAP Solution Architect/SAP Basis job postings. Source: web search and job posting intelligence.

draft.executive_summary: Project Polaris is not a binary cloud versus data center decision. It is a resilience, cost, security, architecture, and operating model decision for a global manufacturer whose Wisconsin data center, plants, SAP estate, hospitality operations, connected products, analytics platforms, and customer digital experiences must continue to perform while the future hosting model is evaluated. Kohler's own public technology priorities point to this conclusion: in January 2026, CTO Eric Tabor connected technology to operational excellence, productivity, and customer value; in August 2025 reporting, Kohler emphasized digital and data capabilities that simplify processes and deepen engagement; and the 2024 Microsoft connected products case study positioned security and privacy as part of brand trust. We propose a 12 week, vendor neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab that gives Kohler board ready evidence, not a disguised migration sales funnel. Workload by workload, we will compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant and edge, and SaaS integration options using dependency data, RTO/RPO requirements, security evidence, network paths, run costs, and operating ownership. Our differentiator is contractual independence: we will not bid downstream hosting, migration, cloud run, DCaaS, or managed services unless Kohler explicitly waives that restriction after the target architecture decision. The result is a Kohler owned decision package that protects manufacturing continuity, preserves choice, supports coexistence with the Cognizant ecosystem, and creates a governed foundation for cloud, data, AI, and digital innovation.
win_themes: Evidence led, vendor neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab comparing public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant and edge, and SaaS integration without forcing a predetermined migration outcome.
Manufacturing safe SAP and enterprise continuity across SAP, VMware/VxRail, Windows/Linux, databases, network, backup/DR, and plant/edge dependencies with rollback gates and RTO/RPO tiering.
Kohler owned decision evidence and no lock in through open book USD TCO, target state architecture, dependency maps, migration waves, compliance evidence, RACI, and downstream implementation package.
Transparent modular commercial model separating advisory assessment, migration, hosting/DCaaS/cloud run, managed services, security/monitoring, backup/DR, and optional transformation waves.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance
sections.content: Kohler needs a target state that can support operational excellence without assuming that every workload belongs in one destination. We will define a vendor neutral reference architecture spanning retained Wisconsin data center capacity, DCaaS/private cloud, public cloud landing zones, hybrid interconnect, plant and edge services, and SaaS integration patterns. The architecture work begins with dependency evidence: SAP, VMware/VxRail, Windows/Linux, databases, integration platforms, analytics, connected product services, backup/DR, IAM, logging, monitoring, contracts, and run costs. Each workload receives a placement rationale, risk rating, security control view, data sensitivity view, and operating owner. Governance is built into the decision process through an architecture board, security and privacy evidence matrix, control ownership RACI, exception register, and FinOps guardrails. Security is treated as connected product brand trust, not only technical compliance: identity, privileged access, encryption, segmentation, vulnerability management, observability, retention, and audit evidence will be mapped by hosting option. This gives Kohler a target state that is executable by any provider, keeps Polaris independent, and turns vendor neutrality into governed enterprise architecture.

The safest transition strategy is one that Kohler can choose only after dependency evidence is visible. During Phase 0, we will not rush to migration execution; we will build the implementation package required to move with confidence when Kohler decides. The team will baseline applications, infrastructure, databases, network paths, batch schedules, SAP touchpoints, Salesforce, CRM, integration, analytics, backup, DR, and plant/edge dependencies, using the prior carve out signal of 400+ applications as a reason to test assumptions workload by workload. We will create migration waves by business criticality, technical affinity, data gravity, licensing constraints, latency, RTO/RPO tier, security posture, and rollback feasibility. Each wave will include entry criteria, rehearsal needs, change windows, hypercare requirements, fallback steps, and decision gates. SAP and enterprise workloads receive specific performance and continuity checks before any recommended hosting move. The output is not a generic migration plan; it is a risk ranked transition strategy that preserves manufacturing continuity, supports low disruption execution, and lets Kohler proceed with any downstream partner.

Polaris depends on connectivity evidence because hosting choices can fail when latency, segmentation, routing, DNS, identity, and plant access are treated as afterthoughts. We will map current network paths among the Wisconsin data center, plants, offices, hospitality environments, public cloud services, DCaaS candidates, SaaS platforms, identity providers, monitoring tools, backup targets, and third party integrations. For each placement option, we will assess bandwidth, latency sensitivity, resilience, routing complexity, firewall and segmentation impact, remote access, private connectivity, internet egress, DNS cutover, and operational ownership. SAP, manufacturing, integration, analytics, and connected product flows will be modeled as end to end service paths rather than isolated servers. The target connectivity blueprint will compare retained data center, DCaaS, public cloud, hybrid, and plant/edge options using the same evidence base, including transition risks and rollback implications. We will also define how network events surface into observability and service integration processes. This keeps the networking strategy aligned to the evidence led Hosting Decision Lab and prevents a hosting recommendation that looks attractive financially but weakens performance, resilience, or supportability.

Kohler's future hosting model must be judged by service continuity, not only by platform features. We will develop an availability, resilience, and disaster recovery model that tiers workloads by business impact, RTO/RPO, data protection needs, recovery sequence, dependency chain, and testability. SAP, databases, integration, analytics, plant/edge, connected product, and customer experience workloads will be reviewed for active dependencies that affect recovery order and rollback decisions. For each hosting option, we will compare high availability patterns, backup architecture, replication approach, recovery location, monitoring coverage, incident escalation, cyber recovery considerations, and evidence required to prove readiness. The DR design will identify where retained Wisconsin data center, DCaaS, public cloud, hybrid/private cloud, and SaaS integration options strengthen or weaken recovery posture. We will document recovery runbooks at the level needed for downstream implementation planning, including test cadence, ownership, success criteria, and known gaps. The result is a resilience decision framework that protects manufacturing operations and customer trust while keeping Kohler in control of the target state.

Cost transparency is a control objective, not a pricing appendix. We will build an open book USD TCO model that compares public cloud, DCaaS, hybrid/private cloud, VMware retain, VMware exit, SAP hosting, backup/DR, network, security, observability, migration effort, operating support, and incumbent coexistence costs. Assumptions will be visible, traceable, and marked by confidence level, with sensitivity analysis for growth, licensing, utilization, reserved capacity, data transfer, resilience tiering, and service coverage. Our commercial model separates Phase 0 advisory assessment from any later migration, hosting/DCaaS/cloud run, managed services, security/monitoring, backup/DR, and optional transformation waves. More importantly, we will contractually commit that we will not bid downstream work unless Kohler explicitly waives the restriction after the board level target architecture decision. That removes the incentive to bias the evaluation toward services we might sell later. This section reinforces the strongest Polaris win theme: Kohler receives decision evidence, commercial comparability, and negotiating leverage without lock in.

A technically sound target state will underperform if the operating model assumes a clean provider takeover. Kohler's likely reality is coexistence across internal teams, the Cognizant ecosystem, infrastructure providers, cloud platforms, DCaaS operators, SaaS vendors, security teams, and plant support. We will define a coexistence first operating model with clear service integration, RACI, escalation paths, incident and problem flow, change governance, access ownership, reporting cadence, and executive decision forums. The model will identify which responsibilities stay with Kohler, which sit with incumbents, which shift to future providers, and which require shared accountability during transition. Observability and AIOps recommendations will focus on integrating signals across tools rather than forcing unnecessary replacement. We will define support requirements for run, transition, hypercare, and DR events, including decision rights and evidence handoffs. The result is a practical operating model that lowers disruption, respects the incumbent landscape, and gives Kohler a reusable governance structure for whichever hosting path the evidence supports.

The Hosting Decision Lab will use tools and methods that produce durable evidence Kohler can retain, inspect, and reuse. We will work from Kohler approved data sources and exportable artifacts: workload inventories, dependency maps, network flows, security control evidence, cost baselines, architecture diagrams, migration waves, RACI models, and FinOps assumptions. The evaluation will cover the platforms already signaled in the scope and prior enterprise context, including SAP, VMware/VxRail, Windows/Linux, databases, backup/DR, network services, public cloud, DCaaS/private cloud, SaaS integration, observability, security monitoring, and plant/edge environments. Tooling recommendations will prioritize interoperability with Kohler and incumbent ecosystems, API or export availability, auditability, and operational adoption. Where automated discovery is available through approved sources, we will use it to accelerate evidence capture; where interviews or workshops are required, we will clearly mark confidence and data gaps. This approach makes platform maturity measurable, keeps decision evidence Kohler owned, and avoids replacing the client's toolchain just to complete an assessment.

Our delivery plan is structured as a 12 week, vendor neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab that maps directly to the expected outputs. Weeks 1 and 2 mobilize governance, confirm scope, issue the data request list, and align stakeholder workshops. Weeks 3 through 5 baseline workloads, SAP and VMware dependencies, network paths, security controls, backup/DR, contracts, service ownership, and run costs. Weeks 6 through 8 create workload placement scorecards across public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration. Weeks 9 and 10 build target architecture, security evidence, resilience tiers, network blueprint, operating model, and migration waves. Weeks 11 and 12 finalize open book USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, RACI, risk register, board ready recommendation, and downstream implementation package. Deliverables will be Kohler owned and provider portable: current state baseline, dependency maps, placement scorecards, architecture diagram, compliance evidence matrix, migration roadmap, rollback gates, and commercial model. This aligns every artifact to the RFP criteria and leaves Kohler with an executable decision package, not another presentation.

critical_issues: The exact recommended price of $14,827,730 is not traceable to the raw data. The knowledge base supports broad envelopes ($5M-$15M+ downstream, $15M-$50M+ or $15M-$75M over 3-5 years, prior recommendations around $29.75M-$30.67M, and a Phase 0 advisory price of $279,533.42), but not this precise figure.

The pricing analysis relies heavily on unsupported or contradicted financial metrics. The raw SEC data says Accenture and TCS financial data are unavailable, and provides only Infosys revenue/margin. Claims about Accenture 17.0% margin, TCS 24.0% margin, TCS revenue/employee, HCLTech 17.2% EBIT margin, Kyndryl 17.7% EBITDA margin, and Ensono revenue/employee are not supported by the supplied sources.
The Infosys operating margin claim is directly contradicted. Agent claims Infosys Q4 FY26 operating margin was 20.9%, while the raw SEC data states Infosys operating margin is 37.22%.

Competitor likelihood assessments are mostly unsupported. The raw data contains no Wisconsin/Kohler competitor awards, no relevant job postings, and no public contract wins for Accenture, TCS, HCLTech, Kyndryl, Ensono, or Infosys in this context.

The win-probability rationale cites specific RFP IDs and deal IDs, including RFP-81E4E8, RFP-3E0EFE, RFP-9F4932, RFP-8D620E, RFP-1A1C8C, RFP-FB30BB, DEAL-003, DEAL-038, and DEAL-094, but those identifiers and outcomes are not present in the raw data.

The claim that Cognizant has a renewed multi-year partnership in core digital foundation domains is not directly supported by the raw data. The knowledge base mentions Cognizant/incumbent scope around AI-driven security, observability, reliability, and operations, but not a renewed multi-year partnership.

The killer differentiator overstates the evidence by claiming none of the six competitors can match vendor-neutrality. The knowledge base supports vendor-neutral positioning as a strategy, but the raw data does not prove competitor inability to match it.
The claim that prior loss evidence shows pricing 20% above competitors can lose is unsupported by the supplied data.
removed_claims: Remove or qualify the exact recommended price of $14,827,730 as unsupported; replace with a sourced range or split Phase 0 advisory pricing from downstream transformation pricing.
Remove the specific RFP identifiers RFP-81E4E8, RFP-3E0EFE, RFP-9F4932, RFP-8D620E, RFP-1A1C8C, and RFP-FB30BB unless those records are provided.
Remove the specific deal references DEAL-003, DEAL-038, and DEAL-094 unless the underlying deal records are provided.
Remove Accenture Q3 FY2026 operating margin of 17.0% and managed-services revenue of $9.39B; Accenture financial data is unavailable in the supplied sources.
Remove TCS Q1 FY2027 revenue, 24.0% operating margin, 593,798 employee count, and $51.4K revenue-per-employee calculation; TCS financial data is unavailable in the supplied sources.
Remove or mark as unsupported the TCS win examples involving a U.S. healthcare payer, U.S. specialty retailer, SKF, and Elopak; these are not in the raw data.
Remove HCLTech FY26 EBIT margin of 17.2%, Wisconsin/Indianapolis job signals, Milwaukee Program Manager claim, and recent data-center win evidence; none are supported by the supplied sources.
Remove Kyndryl FY2026 adjusted EBITDA margin of 17.7%, high-single-digit pretax signing margin, and related pricing-floor calculations; none are supported by the supplied sources.
Remove Ensono $1.1B revenue, 4,000+ associates, $275K revenue-per-associate calculation, and Madison Senior Network Engineer posting; none are supported by the supplied sources.
Correct or remove Infosys Q4 FY26 operating margin of 20.9%; the supplied SEC data says Infosys operating margin is 37.22%.
Remove Infosys Wisconsin SAP/CPG job-posting claims and high likelihood/high confidence conclusion based on those postings; the postings are not in the raw data.
Remove the assertion that none of the six competitors can match the vendor-neutral differentiator; this is an unsupported absolute claim.
Remove the claim that prior loss evidence shows pricing 20% above competitors can lose; no such loss evidence appears in the raw data.

Remove the phrase that Source 2 states no public procurement data is available. The supplied procurement data exists but is not relevant to Kohler/Wisconsin/these competitors; state that no relevant public procurement award data was supplied instead.
warnings: The knowledge base strongly supports solution fit for Kohler Project Polaris: vendor-neutral 12-week Phase 0, workload-by-workload hosting evaluation, SAP/VMware/physical server dependency mapping, open-book USD TCO, landing-zone architecture, migration waves, DR, RACI, and operating model.
The knowledge base does not provide award outcomes or proof that the bidder won similar Kohler work, so win probability should be reduced from 42% to about 34%.
The supplied procurement records are unrelated UK and U.S. federal awards and do not validate competitor pricing, Wisconsin award patterns, or Kohler procurement behavior.
Cloud pricing data is empty, so cloud/DCaaS/hosting price estimates cannot be treated as high-confidence market-priced outputs.
Competitor pricing ranges are largely speculative buyer-envelope estimates, not evidence-based bid estimates.
Be careful using the word 'requires.' The raw knowledge base describes recurring pursuit-record requirements and evaluation categories, but it is not the original Kohler RFP text.

The analysis should distinguish clearly between a Phase 0 advisory engagement, where one sourced prior recommendation is $279,533.42, and a downstream migration/hosting/managed-services opportunity, where sourced ranges are much larger.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Position the response as an objective, low-risk hosting-options evaluation that gives Kohler an evidence-based path from its Wisconsin data center footprint to the right mix of cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid services. Win by combining enterprise-grade architecture and compliance evidence with manufacturing-aware transition planning, transparent economics, and a practical operating model that preserves SAP, database, network, and plant continuity. Treat this as a strategic wedge into the downstream migration and managed-services contract, not just a standalone assessment.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: The single most important thing to win is to demonstrate that we can give Kohler a credible, unbiased, commercially transparent path to exit or reduce its data center footprint without disrupting manufacturing operations, SAP/database performance, security/compliance, or global business continuity.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid pricing approach: competitive and transparent for the Phase 0 evaluation, scenario-based TCO for Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options, and value-based positioning for the downstream data center exit and managed-services opportunity. Provide USD pricing with clear assumptions, dependencies, exclusions, unit rates, 3-year and 5-year run-rate views, migration/implementation options, managed-service tiers, DR/backup/network/security cost components, FinOps optimization levers, and sensitivity ranges tied to workload counts and consumption. Avoid a single opaque fixed price; Kohler is explicitly evaluating cost and commercial transparency, and the missing inventory means pricing should be modular, auditable, and adjustable as discovery validates volumes.
key_risks: This is a Phase 0 evaluation and may not commit Kohler to implementation, so the pursuit must avoid over-investing in a full transformation proposal while still positioning for the downstream migration/managed-services award.

Missing attachments create significant compliance risk: the Vendor Response template, Evaluation Template, inventory dataset, and online RFP package may contain additional mandatory questions, scoring weights, workload volumes, pricing tabs, or hidden disqualifiers.

Certification and compliance evidence is an eliminatory requirement, but the RFP excerpt does not list exact standards; downstream teams must provide broad evidence and explicitly map certifications to cloud, DCaaS, hybrid, regions, and managed services.

Commercial evaluation will likely scrutinize transparency because no budget or baseline is provided; vague pricing, bundled assumptions, non-USD values, or payment terms worse than net 90 could materially weaken the bid.

Manufacturing and OT-adjacent connectivity introduces hidden transition risk: plant latency, uptime windows, read-only/controlled integrations, network segmentation, and business continuity must be addressed without overstating OT migration scope.

The workload estate is broad and potentially under-discovered, including SAP HANA, Oracle, SQL Server, file storage, backup/DR, middleware, IAM, monitoring, and security tooling; inaccurate sizing assumptions could damage credibility and pricing.

Large competitors may have incumbent relationships or existing cloud/SAP/managed-services relationships with Kohler; client intelligence must identify incumbency before final win themes and pricing are locked.

A single preferred answer may not be obvious because Kohler is asking for Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options; a biased cloud-only or DCaaS-only response may score poorly against the objective evaluation intent.
Response quality risk is high because simple Yes/No answers are explicitly insufficient; every requirement needs a detailed, structured, evidence-backed narrative aligned to the template.

Confidentiality and third-party disclosure restrictions may limit use of subcontractors, hyperscaler solution architects, or tooling vendors unless proper consent and information controls are in place.

gaps: WIN_INTEL lacks outcome-backed comparable wins/losses and uses the current RFP as a similar deal, which weakens probability calibration.
No visible, reliable win-probability model tying similar deals, incumbent threat, disqualifiers, and capability gaps together.
Competitor intelligence shown focuses on Accenture and does not visibly address Cognizant as a likely incumbent/strategic partner, despite strong client-intel evidence.
Competitor pricing ranges are acknowledged as low-confidence and are mostly anchored to inferred opportunity size and corporate margin, not comparable deal economics.
Full competitor output appears truncated, so it is unclear whether all likely bidders and vulnerabilities were assessed.
Capability gaps and disqualifier impact are not clearly reflected in the visible Phase 2 outputs.
strengths: CLIENT_INTEL contains specific, non-generic signals from web research rather than only RFP inference.
Client unstated needs are actionable: workload placement, hybrid operating model, app dependency mapping, observability/AIOps, cyber/privacy-by-design, SAP ECC path, vendor governance, and FinOps.
Client-intel narrative is aligned to Kohler’s apparent strategic moves around Cognizant, AI/cloud operations, Microsoft security, connected products, and complex enterprise application landscape.
Competitor analysis for Accenture is reasonably caveated and does not overclaim confirmed account access.
RFP disqualifiers are clearly captured and operationally useful for proposal compliance.

agentic_decisions.reasoning: Position the response as an objective, low-risk hosting-options evaluation that gives Kohler an evidence-based path from its Wisconsin data center footprint to the right mix of cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid services. Win by combining enterprise-grade architecture and compliance evidence with manufacturing-aware transition planning, transparent economics, and a practical operating model that preserves SAP, database, network, and plant continuity. Treat this as a strategic wedge into the downstream migration and managed-services contract, not just a standalone assessment.

The client intelligence is strong enough to proceed: it contains specific web-derived signals tied to Kohler’s recent Cognizant partnership, CDO priorities, Microsoft security/collaboration work, connected-product trust, app complexity, SAP ECC exposure, and hybrid/cloud operating model needs. However, the win-intel and competitor intelligence have material weaknesses that could directly affect win strategy. WIN_INTEL appears to use the current RFP itself and a likely internally generated Phase 0 proposal as 'similar deals,' both with no outcome, which does not provide real win/loss calibration. That makes any win probability or recommended pursuit narrative weak if based on this evidence. COMPETITOR output shown is detailed for Accenture, but the visible evidence is not enough: pricing is mostly anchored to the inferred RFP size and corporate margin rather than comparable hosting/DCaaS deals, and the output does not visibly prioritize Cognizant even though client intel indicates Cognizant is a recent/active Kohler strategic partner across AI, cloud, operations, data centers, end-user services, and cyber. Missing or underweighting Cognizant would materially weaken the proposal’s competitive positioning.
